technical_documents.description: Focuses sunlight via a Fresnel lens array onto a Stirling engine displacer piston, generating mechanical shaft power for milling, pumping, or electricity.
technical_documents.problem_statement: Rural grain mills depend heavily on erratic, carbon-intensive diesel supply chains; a single 50 kg sack of maize costs up to 3 USD in fuel to mill, which erodes over 40% of smallholder farmer profit margins and triggers regional food insecurity due to fluctuating processing overheads.
technical_documents.engineering_solution: A dual 1.2m Fresnel lens array concentrates 800W of direct normal irradiation (DNI) thermal energy onto the high-temperature stainless steel hot cap of a gamma-configuration Stirling engine. This creates a steep thermal differential (\u0394T > 300\u00b0C) against the ambient cold-sink cylinder, expanding the internal gas working fluid to deliver 120W of continuous mechanical shaft power capable of driving a commercial agricultural hammer mill at 400 rpm via a 3:1 pulley belt drive system.

step_by_step_build: Step 1: Assemble the Stirling engine kit per manufacturer instructions; lap the power piston cylinder for a tight seal.
Step 2: Mount Fresnel lenses on the tracking frame oriented south (northern hemisphere) at latitude angle.
Step 3: Position the engine so the hot cap sits precisely at the focal point (approx. 600mm from lens).
Step 4: Attach flywheel and belt drive to mill input shaft; adjust tension for 3:1 speed reduction.
Step 5: Align tracking arm for manual hourly adjustment or connect a simple clockwork auto-tracker.

comments.text: Mills 10 kg maize per hour on a sunny day. Total game changer for our village cooperative.
What's the startup time from cold? Considering this for intermittent cloud conditions.
